extractor.lens: Record lifecycles, organizational handoffs, decision authority, exception recovery, stable identity, offline conflict, correction, supersession, and uncertainty

evidence.anchor: source/Vineyard-Workflow-Discovery-report.md#L81-L87 — Workflow card: seasonal work planning and work-order dispatch
evidence.scope: Planning, dispatch, execution feedback, and office reconciliation are a composite workflow; the report says the work-order label is not a universal documented SOP.

evidence.anchor: source/Vineyard-Workflow-Discovery-report.md#L89-L95 — Workflow card: scouting to spray execution and compliance record
evidence.scope: Monitoring, treatment constraints, execution, application records, posting, reporting, and efficacy follow-up are explicitly documented.

evidence.anchor: source/Vineyard-Workflow-Discovery-report.md#L97-L101 — Workflow card: irrigation scheduling and fertility adjustment
evidence.scope: Agronomic inputs and outputs are well supported; exact field-to-office choreography is less directly documented.
Repeated estimation from block counts, sample weights, and history supports winery, labor, bin, and financial planning.

evidence.anchor: source/Vineyard-Workflow-Discovery-report.md#L103-L107 — Workflow card: crop estimation and maturity tracking to harvest-date decision
evidence.scope: The broad estimate-to-sample-to-pick loop is strongly supported; buyer-specific thresholds remain variable.

evidence.anchor: source/Vineyard-Workflow-Discovery-report.md#L109-L115 — Workflow card: harvest execution to weigh-ticket and payment reconciliation
evidence.scope: Load identification, intake, weigh documentation, quality disposition, correction, and settlement form a directly documented cross-organization chain.

evidence.anchor: source/Vineyard-Workflow-Discovery-report.md#L70-L70 — Crew scheduling, attendance, task verification, and payroll allocation
evidence.scope: Labor, safety, pay, and verification consequences are supported; the precise operational closeout sequence is not directly observed.

evidence.anchor: source/Vineyard-Workflow-Discovery-report.md#L56-L56 — Operations coordination is often hidden inside other titles
evidence.scope: Materials, equipment, repair, payroll support, and record routing appear as responsibilities distributed across mixed roles.

evidence.anchor: source/Vineyard-Workflow-Discovery-report.md#L29-L29 — A key practical distinction is between estate-grown blocks and contract fruit
evidence.scope: Contract fruit adds an interorganizational relationship loop around identity, requirements, sampling, timing, and settlement.

evidence.anchor: source/Vineyard-Workflow-Discovery-report.md#L58-L58 — Compliance and sustainability personnel are best understood as workflow integrators
evidence.scope: Written plans, annual evidence, audit findings, certification withholding, and corrective action are directly supported.

evidence.anchor: source/Vineyard-Workflow-Discovery-report.md#L15-L17 — Vineyard operating model and blurry role boundaries
evidence.scope: Field, office, management, and outside parties converge at the operational middle layer, with substantial role overlap.

evidence.anchor: source/Vineyard-Workflow-Discovery-report.md#L9-L9 — The operational burden increases sharply when the same block is named differently
evidence.scope: Block splits, renames, combinations, and cross-system aliases break comparison, history, and compliance.

evidence.anchor: source/Vineyard-Workflow-Discovery-report.md#L46-L46 — Event-driven exceptions can override the calendar
evidence.scope: Frost, smoke, and heat create urgent replanning, safety, sampling, contract, and harvest branches.

evidence.anchor: source/Vineyard-Workflow-Discovery-report.md#L138-L149 — Exception inventory and digital opportunity map
evidence.scope: Operational exceptions expose weak identity, stale information, duplicate records, and late evidence assembly.

evidence.anchor: source/Vineyard-Workflow-Discovery-report.md#L151-L153 — The largest unknowns are behavioral rather than structural
evidence.scope: Verbal closure, paper-first capture, contradictory-number reconciliation, pick-date calls, and post-submission corrections require observation.
Organic and biodynamic regimes add prechecks, separation, inventory, training, application, transport, inspection, and evidence obligations.

evidence.anchor: source/Vineyard-Workflow-Discovery-report.md#L54-L54 — Grower-relations work sits at the boundary between viticulture, procurement, quality assurance, and operations
evidence.scope: Grower relations connects vineyard observation and sampling with contract terms, winery decisions, delivery, quality disposition, and payment.

evidence.anchor: source/Vineyard-Workflow-Discovery-report.md#L117-L130 — A synthesized handoff map for the two most important loops
evidence.scope: Observation becomes interpretation, decision, assignment, execution, completion evidence, downstream reconciliation, and future planning.

workflows.name: Seasonal planning, dispatch, execution, and verification
workflows.purpose: Convert seasonal objectives and current field signals into accountable block-level work, then preserve execution, exception, actual-quantity, and verification history for downstream use.
workflows.frequency: Daily during active field operations, with seasonal planning resets
triggers: A recurring operation reaches its planned window
preconditions: The work target resolves to a stable operational block
The triggering signal is attributable and current
Restrictions and required resources are known or explicitly marked unknown

steps.description: A manager or viticulturist records a seasonal need or field signal against the stable block identity.
The observation is translated into a proposed method, target, timing window, and completion standard.
The manager resolves priority, restrictions, budget, and resource conflicts before releasing the work.
The approved work is assigned to an accountable foreman, crew, or contractor with block, method, and timing context.
The field lead acknowledges the assignment and confirms that instructions, safety conditions, and resources are actionable.
The crew executes the work and preserves progress, resource use, and exception context at the block or row level.
The field lead reports actual acreage or quantity, labor, equipment time, completion status, and unresolved exceptions.
The manager verifies scope and actuals before closure data flows to progress, payroll, compliance, and costing records.

decisions.question: Does the signal require action now, later, monitoring only, or no action?
Can this work be released given priority, restrictions, resources, and approval limits?
Is the reported result complete, partial, blocked, or inconsistent with the assignment?

outputs: Progress, payroll, compliance, and cost inputs
handoffs: Vineyard manager to operations coordinator or foreman
Field completion back to vineyard manager
Verified actuals to payroll, compliance, and finance
approvals: Routine release authority depends on role and organization
Restricted, high-cost, contracted, or harvest work requires stronger approval
workarounds: Verbal or phone dispatch followed by later record creation
Split remaining work without losing the original authorization
Correct actuals while retaining prior values
risks: Downstream payroll, compliance, or cost mismatch
source_anchors: source/Vineyard-Workflow-Discovery-report.md#L81-L87 — Workflow card: seasonal work planning and work-order dispatch
field_validation_questions: Which routine assignments require explicit approval in each organization?
How are partial work, verbal closure, and changed priorities recorded today?
Who may correct or supersede verified completion quantities?

workflows.name: Scouting, recommendation, crop protection, and application records
workflows.purpose: Move a pest or disease signal through diagnosis, treatment authorization, safe application, defensible recording, restriction communication, and efficacy follow-up.
workflows.frequency: Scheduled monitoring with event-driven escalation throughout the season
triggers: Trap or weather risk crosses a review threshold
A certification or buyer review exposes a gap
preconditions: The affected location and observation time are known
The proposed material is permitted for the block and buyer
Weather, calibration, worker protection, REI, and PHI checks are current

steps.description: A scout records pressure, symptoms, trap results, or weather risk against the block and monitoring context.
The viticulturist confirms pressure and documents whether a treatment threshold and target are supported.
The authorized owner checks product, buyer, certification, weather, worker, REI, PHI, and sensitive-area constraints.
The application is scheduled and assigned with location, material, rate, method, PPE, posting, and restriction instructions.
The applicator rechecks field conditions, executes the application, and preserves actual product, rate, weather, method, and operator data.
Application facts are entered into the spray diary and required jurisdictional, buyer, worker-access, or certification records.
The viticulturist performs the scheduled efficacy follow-up and records outcome, retreatment need, or corrected diagnosis.

decisions.question: Is the observed pressure confirmed and above the operational treatment threshold?
Do product, weather, worker, buyer, certification, REI, and PHI constraints permit application?
Is the application record complete and consistent across required destinations?

handoffs: Application restriction to workers and managers
approvals: Treatment and material authorization
Exception approval for alternatives or rescheduling
Correction approval for submitted records where required
exceptions: Worker presence in an exclusion zone
workarounds: Reschedule or split a block
Select an allowed alternative product
Create buyer-specific records from one source entry
Correct a submitted record without deleting its prior version
source_anchors: source/Vineyard-Workflow-Discovery-report.md#L89-L95 — Workflow card: scouting to spray execution and compliance record
field_validation_questions: Who holds application approval authority for routine and exception cases?
Which destinations require duplicate or buyer-specific diary formats?
How are late corrections to already submitted pesticide records authorized and transmitted?

workflows.name: Irrigation scheduling, fertility adjustment, and delivery verification
workflows.purpose: Translate plant, soil, weather, phenology, and nutrient evidence into authorized water or fertility actions, then verify actual delivery and retain environmental and operational totals.
workflows.frequency: Recurring irrigation cycles with event-driven adjustments
triggers: A heat event or weather shift occurs
Uneven delivery or a system malfunction is reported
preconditions: Monitoring data has a time and block context
System availability and restrictions are known
Fertility inputs are checked against recent applications and certification requirements

steps.description: The viticulturist or irrigation lead reviews block, weather, plant, soil, and recent-delivery evidence.
A water-duration, timing, or fertility adjustment is documented with its evidence and intended outcome.
The manager confirms resource, system, labor, certification, and competing-operation constraints.
The authorized set or application is assigned with block, valve or zone, duration, quantity, and verification expectation.
The irrigator or applicator starts delivery and records the actual start, system condition, and any interruption.
Actual duration, quantity, affected zones, and observed malfunctions or uneven delivery are closed against the assignment.
The irrigation lead verifies delivery against plan and routes water and nitrogen totals to operating and sustainability histories.

decisions.question: Should water or nutrient delivery increase, decrease, continue, defer, or require more evidence?
Can the recommendation proceed given system, labor, resource, and compliance constraints?
Does actual delivery match the authorized block, zone, duration, and quantity?

handoffs: Sensor, lab, or irrigator observation to viticulturist
Manager or irrigation lead to irrigator
Verified totals to compliance and management
approvals: Manager approval for material, resource, or cross-operation conflicts
Certification-sensitive fertility approval where applicable
exceptions: Nutrient input conflicts with certification rules
workarounds: Use field inspection while marking missing telemetry
Split an irrigation set by working zones
Assign repair and retain the interrupted delivery history
Correct actuals after meter reconciliation
risks: Office assumptions diverge from field delivery
source_anchors: source/Vineyard-Workflow-Discovery-report.md#L97-L101 — Workflow card: irrigation scheduling and fertility adjustment
field_validation_questions: What constitutes proof of actual delivery in operations without reliable meters?
Who may alter a set after it has begun?
How are interrupted or uneven sets reconciled to water and nitrogen totals?

workflows.name: Crop estimation and forecast revision
workflows.purpose: Produce versioned block-level yield estimates, communicate their uncertainty and changes, and reconcile forecast versions with harvest actuals for planning and future estimation.
workflows.frequency: Repeated at defined crop stages and whenever material variation emerges
triggers: Lag-phase or véraison estimate window begins
Winery or finance requests an update
preconditions: The sampled population and method are attributable
Historical records resolve to the same operational block or a documented predecessor
Assumptions and missing data remain visible

steps.description: Representative vines and clusters are sampled with block, date, method, count, and variation context.
The viticulturist calculates a forecast using current samples, block population, and relevant historical weights.
The manager reviews estimate range, confidence, material changes, and downstream capacity or contract implications.
The approved forecast version and uncertainty are communicated to winery, finance, grower, and labor-planning consumers as applicable.
Material new evidence creates a revised forecast while preserving the prior version, reason, author, and recipients.
The revised forecast becomes current and explicitly supersedes the prior communicated forecast without erasing it.
Harvest actuals are compared with forecast versions and retained as evidence for future block-level assumptions.

decisions.question: Is the sample representative enough to support a block forecast?
Does the forecast require crop adjustment or a downstream planning change?
outcomes: Notify winery or finance of material variance

decisions.question: Is new evidence material enough to supersede the communicated forecast?

outputs: Labor, bin, winery, and financial planning input
handoffs: Manager or grower relations to winery and finance
Forecast change to labor and logistics planning
approvals: Manager approval for externally communicated forecast or crop adjustment
Contract-specific communication authority where applicable
workarounds: Stratify a variable block
Publish a range with explicit low confidence
Manually map historical aliases
Issue a corrected version and preserve prior recipients
risks: Stale forecast drives labor or winery commitments
source_anchors: source/Vineyard-Workflow-Discovery-report.md#L103-L107 — crop estimation and maturity tracking to harvest-date decision
field_validation_questions: Which forecast versions are contractually or operationally binding?
What variance triggers mandatory recommunication?
How are block splits and blended harvest actuals reconciled to historical forecasts?

workflows.name: Maturity sampling, winery communication, and pick decision
workflows.purpose: Connect attributable field samples, laboratory and sensory evidence, changing weather and capacity constraints, and cross-organization authority to one current pick decision and schedule commitment.
workflows.frequency: Weekly after véraison, increasing to daily or near-daily as harvest approaches
triggers: A proposed pick window needs authorization
A prior result becomes stale or disputed
preconditions: Sample identity resolves to the intended block
Result date, method, and laboratory provenance are known
Final pick authority and required participants are explicit

steps.description: A representative berry or cluster sample is collected and linked to block, date, method, collector, and chain of custody.
Laboratory values and field condition observations are returned against the same sample identity and made available for interpretation.
The viticulturist interprets chemistry, sensory condition, trend, weather, and crop goals into a readiness recommendation.
Grower, manager, and winery resolve quality, contract authority, weather, labor, delivery slot, and tank-capacity constraints.
The authorized block, pick window, quantity expectation, and delivery target become an accountable scheduling commitment.

decisions.question: Is the sample identity, method, timing, and coverage sufficient for interpretation?
Is evidence sufficient to recommend picking, continued hang time, or intervention?
Who has final authority and can the intended pick window be committed?

outputs: Labor, container, truck, and winery scheduling input
handoffs: Laboratory to viticulturist and grower relations
Viticulturist to manager, grower, and winemaker
Committed window to labor, trucking, and intake
approvals: Pick authority may sit with the winery under contract
Shared communication and mutual agreement remain operationally important
exceptions: Rapid rot, smoke, or weather shift
Decision communicated to only part of the chain
workarounds: Increase sample frequency
Collect paired or witnessed samples
Negotiate a conditional pick window
Use calls or email and reconcile the durable decision later
risks: Pick decision based on stale evidence
Crew or truck commitment before winery acceptance
source_anchors: source/Vineyard-Workflow-Discovery-report.md#L103-L107 — crop estimation and maturity tracking to harvest-date decision
field_validation_questions: At what age does each maturity result become operationally stale?
How is final pick authority represented across contract variants?
What evidence resolves disagreement between vineyard and winery measurements?

workflows.name: Harvest planning, execution, loads, intake, quality, and reconciliation
workflows.purpose: Move fruit from an authorized block and pick window through accountable harvest, load identity, transport, winery intake, quality disposition, weigh documentation, and commercial reconciliation.
workflows.frequency: Per pick authorization, harvest shift, load, intake event, and settlement review
triggers: Quality disposition or weight differs from expectation
Settlement evidence is incomplete or disputed
preconditions: The pick is authorized by the accountable party
Block, grower, variety, and delivery destination are resolvable
Crew, containers, transport, and intake slot are committed

steps.description: The authorized pick becomes a coordinated crew, equipment, container, truck, block, and winery-slot plan.
The harvest lead briefs the crew and harvests the identified block under agreed time, temperature, quality, and quantity conditions.
Each physical load receives a block, grower, variety, pick, container, and transport identity before leaving the vineyard.
The driver transports the identified load and preserves delivery timing and chain-of-custody context.
Winery intake matches load identity, weighs and samples the fruit, and records acceptance, downgrade, or rejection.
The weigh bill, quality result, delivery record, and disposition are transmitted and entered within the required window.
Grower or manager compares the entered weight, quality, block, and terms with retained field and weigh documentation.
Finance uses the reconciled delivery and contract terms for settlement while retaining adjustments and disputes.

decisions.question: Can the authorized pick be executed with the committed crew, containers, transport, and winery slot?
Does the delivered fruit meet identity, timing, condition, and quality tolerances?
Do weigh, quality, identity, and contract records agree sufficiently for settlement?

handoffs: Winery or manager to harvest operations
Intake disposition to grower relations and manager
approvals: Final pick authorization
Winery quality disposition
Commercial adjustment or dispute settlement
exceptions: Hot fruit or material other than grapes
workarounds: Alternate delivery by agreement
Emergency sorting
Conditional hold
Repricing or downgrade negotiation
Manual weigh-entry correction
Alternate buyer where notice allows
risks: Fruit delivered from the wrong block
source_anchors: source/Vineyard-Workflow-Discovery-report.md#L109-L115 — Workflow card: harvest execution to weigh-ticket and payment reconciliation
field_validation_questions: Which physical and digital identifiers travel with each load?
Who can amend an intake or weigh entry after submission?
What timelines and escalation paths govern downgrade, rejection, and settlement disputes?

workflows.name: Crew scheduling, attendance, time, payroll, and cost allocation
workflows.purpose: Connect authorized work and crew instructions to attributable attendance, task progress, verified labor quantities, compliant payroll inputs, and block-level cost allocation without hiding later corrections.
workflows.frequency: Per shift, crew, task, and payroll cutoff
triggers: A crew changes blocks or tasks
Time, piece, or cost allocation is disputed
preconditions: Workers and accountable employer or contractor are identifiable
Instructions are understandable and acknowledged
Block, task, pay basis, and cost destination are known or explicitly unresolved

steps.description: The operations coordinator schedules an identified crew against authorized block work, timing, instructions, and pay basis.
The foreman delivers and confirms task, block, safety, language, and completion instructions before work begins.
Workers check in and labor is attributed to the current crew, block, task, employer, and pay context.
The foreman closes attendance, time or piece quantities, task changes, progress, breaks, and unresolved exceptions for the shift.
A supervisor verifies worker, block, task, quantity, contractor, and completion consistency before payroll cutoff.
Payroll and finance allocate verified labor to pay and cost destinations and return mismatches for attributed correction.
The approved payroll export and cost allocation are retained with the verified source and any superseded versions.

decisions.question: Is the crew qualified, available, and able to understand the assigned work and safety requirements?
How should time or pieces be split when a crew changes blocks, tasks, or completion status?
Are labor quantities and allocations complete enough for payroll and costing?

handoffs: Manager or operations coordinator to foreman
Verified labor to payroll and finance
Payroll exception back to field supervision
approvals: Supervisor verification before payroll cutoff
Authorized correction after export or payment
workarounds: Paper roster or verbal closure followed by later entry
Allocate unresolved time temporarily and flag for review
Correct a prior export with an explicit supersession link
source_anchors: source/Vineyard-Workflow-Discovery-report.md#L70-L70 — Crew scheduling, attendance, task verification, and payroll allocation
source/Vineyard-Workflow-Discovery-report.md#L151-L153 — behavioral unknowns and field interview guide
field_validation_questions: Which records originate on paper, verbally, or in contractor systems?
How are task and block switches captured during a shift?
Who can correct verified or exported labor, and how do payroll and cost systems receive that correction?

workflows.name: Materials, purchasing, equipment readiness, and repair
workflows.purpose: Turn a field or seasonal resource need into approved, available, staged, and accountable material or equipment readiness while preserving shortages, substitutions, failures, repairs, and verification.
workflows.frequency: Continuous readiness review with task-driven requests and urgent repair exceptions
triggers: Authorized work lacks a required resource
Equipment inspection or operation finds a defect
A certification constraint changes allowed material
preconditions: The request identifies its intended work or operational purpose
Allowed substitutions and approval limits are known
Equipment identity and safety state are attributable

steps.description: A coordinator, foreman, or operator identifies a material shortage, readiness gap, inspection issue, or equipment failure tied to planned work.
The need is translated into a specification, quantity, timing, allowed substitution, repair scope, and operational impact.
The manager checks cost, urgency, supplier, certification, safety, and approval limits before authorizing purchase or repair.
The approved purchase, transfer, staging action, or repair is assigned to a vendor, coordinator, or mechanic.
Materials are received and staged or equipment is repaired with actual quantity, condition, location, cost, and exception evidence.
The accountable field lead verifies that the resource is safe, allowed, sufficient, and ready for the intended work.
Receipt, issue, repair, usage, and cost records are matched to the approved request and work context.

decisions.question: Does the resource gap block work, reduce scope, or permit a safe temporary alternative?
Can purchase, transfer, substitution, or repair be authorized within cost and compliance limits?
Is the material or equipment safe, allowed, sufficient, and ready for release?

handoffs: Foreman or operator to operations coordinator
Coordinator to manager or finance approver
Vendor or mechanic back to field lead
Verified receipt or repair to finance and work planning
approvals: Purchase and contractor approval by threshold
Allowed substitution approval
Safety release after repair
exceptions: Resource assigned to the wrong block or task
workarounds: Transfer from another site
Use an approved substitute
Split work around available equipment
Emergency repair followed by formal documentation
risks: Lost linkage between failure and interrupted work
source_anchors: source/Vineyard-Workflow-Discovery-report.md#L56-L56 — Operations coordination is often hidden inside other titles
source/Vineyard-Workflow-Discovery-report.md#L72-L72 — Purchasing, materials staging, and equipment readiness
field_validation_questions: Which readiness checks are formal versus verbal?
What approval thresholds and emergency exceptions exist?
How are material substitutions and repaired-equipment release documented today?

workflows.name: Grower onboarding, contract-fruit coordination, and relationship history
workflows.purpose: Establish and maintain a shared operational identity, requirements, authority, communication, quality, delivery, and dispute context across grower and winery organizations before and throughout the season.
workflows.frequency: Onboarding and annual renewal, then recurring visits, reports, changes, delivery, and dispute events
triggers: A grower or block enters consideration
A contract or annual requirement changes
A block is split, renamed, or remapped
A report or vineyard visit is due
A quality, delivery, or payment dispute occurs
preconditions: Both organizations and accountable contacts are identifiable
The physical harvested unit can be mapped across aliases
Contract and operational authority are not silently inferred

steps.description: Grower relations records the candidate grower, blocks, contacts, history, operational fit, and unresolved due-diligence questions.
Physical blocks and their grower, winery, compliance, and historical aliases are reconciled into a stable identity map.
Authorized parties agree contract, quality, reporting, spray, sampling, pick, delivery, downgrade, dispute, and payment terms.
Contacts, visit cadence, reporting obligations, sample routes, decision authority, and delivery instructions are assigned across organizations.
Required maps, histories, specifications, contacts, and reporting channels are checked before high-pressure harvest coordination begins.
A changed block, term, authority, specification, or relationship issue is documented with impact and proposed amendment.
The approved amendment becomes current while the prior identity mapping, term, and recipient history remain available.

decisions.question: Is available block, spray, certification, quality, and relationship evidence sufficient to continue onboarding?
Are operational responsibilities and final authorities explicit enough for both organizations?
Does the change require an amendment, temporary exception, suspension, or relationship closure?

handoffs: Grower relations to viticulture, compliance, winery, intake, and finance
Grower reports and samples to winery
Delivery and quality disposition to relationship and settlement history
approvals: Contract and amendment authority
Final pick authority where assigned to the winery
Downgrade, rejection, and commercial dispute authority
workarounds: Manual map-book reconciliation
Conditional onboarding with explicit evidence due
Phone or email decision followed by durable amendment
Temporary operational exception with expiration
risks: Two organizations act on different block identities
Dispute context is lost across seasons
source_anchors: source/Vineyard-Workflow-Discovery-report.md#L73-L73 — Grower onboarding, contract administration, and relationship history
field_validation_questions: Which block identity is authoritative at each organization and stage?
How are informal relationship commitments converted into contract amendments?
What history must survive staff turnover, annual renewal, suspension, or grower exit?

workflows.name: Certification, audit preparation, corrective action, and evidence
workflows.purpose: Turn regime-specific plans and ordinary operating records into complete audit evidence, route findings into accountable corrective action, and retain certification, withholding, correction, and supersession history.
workflows.frequency: Continuous evidence capture with annual submissions, periodic audits, and event-driven corrective actions
triggers: Annual plan or questionnaire is due
An audit or inspection is scheduled
preconditions: Certification regime, site, organization, and block scope are explicit
Evidence retains source, author, date, and applicable standard
Missing evidence is visible before submission

steps.description: The compliance coordinator maps regime requirements and annual deadlines to sites, blocks, records, and accountable owners.
Ordinary operating evidence and regime-specific plans, logs, maps, questionnaires, and attestations are assembled with provenance.
The coordinator checks completeness, consistency, block scope, signatures, and unresolved gaps before submission.
The accountable manager approves the evidence package and any disclosed gap or temporary action plan.
The certifier or auditor reviews submitted evidence and field conditions and records compliance or a specific finding.
A finding is translated into an owner, due date, required remediation, and acceptable closure evidence.
The operational owner completes remediation without detaching it from the original finding and affected records.
Corrective evidence is resubmitted with the finding, prior evidence, changed record, and responsible person linked.
The certifier accepts closure and issues or restores the applicable certification status, or keeps certification withheld.

decisions.question: Which regimes, sites, blocks, operating records, and special proofs are in scope?
Is evidence complete and defensible enough to submit without concealing known gaps?
What correction and proof will close the finding within the allowed deadline?

handoffs: Field and office record owners to compliance coordinator
Certification status to winery customer and management
approvals: Management approval of submissions and disclosed gaps
Certifier acceptance of corrective action and certification status
exceptions: Record does not resolve to certified block
Organic buffer or cleaning evidence absent
workarounds: Assemble evidence manually from field and office systems
Submit corrective action and supplemental evidence
Keep affected crop or activity separate
Retain prior record and issue an attributed correction
source_anchors: source/Vineyard-Workflow-Discovery-report.md#L58-L58 — compliance and sustainability personnel as workflow integrators
field_validation_questions: Which requirements can be derived from ordinary operating records and which need separate evidence?
How are findings assigned and escalated across staff or contractor boundaries?
How do corrected records propagate to prior submissions and buyer-specific packages?

overlays.name: Stable block and record identity
overlays.operational_rule: A physical operational unit retains one stable identity while organization-specific names, splits, merges, renames, contracts, and predecessor-successor relationships remain versioned aliases rather than destructive replacements.
exceptions: Two records claim the same alias
A load or sample references an unknown alias
A block split or merge is only known in one organization
recovery: Retain the prior mapping and correction history
field_validation_questions: Who may assert or approve a cross-organization identity mapping?
Which historical splits and merges must remain queryable?

overlays.name: Exception, escalation, and accountable recovery
overlays.operational_rule: An exception preserves the interrupted state, affected scope, observed facts, owner, escalation target, decision, partial result, and explicit resume, cancel, correct, or supersede outcome.
exceptions: Partial safe work is confused with whole-task completion
An exception is closed without evidence
recovery: Record emergency action after the fact with attribution
field_validation_questions: Which exception types require immediate external notification?
Who can release each blocked state?

overlays.name: Permission, approval, and decision authority
overlays.operational_rule: Recommendation, approval, assignment, execution, verification, correction, and commercial acceptance remain separately attributable even when one person performs multiple roles.
exceptions: Contract authority differs from operational practice
Approver is unavailable during an urgent window
A decision is communicated but not durably recorded
recovery: Record emergency authority and later ratification
source_anchors: source/Vineyard-Workflow-Discovery-report.md#L17-L17 — role boundaries are clear in theory and blurry in practice
source/Vineyard-Workflow-Discovery-report.md#L42-L42 — final pick authority may sit with the winery
field_validation_questions: Which roles can approve, delegate, correct, and supersede each consequential record?
Where is separation of duties mandatory?

overlays.name: Notification, acknowledgement, and stale-information control
overlays.operational_rule: A consequential message identifies the source record and version, intended recipients, urgency, response expectation, acknowledgement, expiry or stale time, and escalation path.
exceptions: Only some parties receive a changed decision
A verbal acknowledgement is not reconciled
recovery: Require acknowledgement for safety or commercial decisions
source_anchors: source/Vineyard-Workflow-Discovery-report.md#L7-L11 — timely updates and fragile record chains
field_validation_questions: Which messages require positive acknowledgement?
How long may each observation, lab result, restriction, or pick instruction remain current?

overlays.name: Offline capture, synchronization, and conflict
overlays.operational_rule: Field records distinguish observed time from captured and synchronized time, retain device or source provenance, and never silently overwrite a concurrent office or cross-organization version.
exceptions: Deleted or renamed target while offline
source_anchors: source/Vineyard-Workflow-Discovery-report.md#L151-L153 — behavioral unknowns around verbal, paper-first, contradictory, and corrected records
field_validation_questions: Which workflows are actually captured offline or on paper?
What conflict frequency, latency, device sharing, and clock behavior occurs in the field?

overlays.name: Seasonal hazard override
overlays.operational_rule: Frost, smoke, heat, and acute weather events may preempt the seasonal plan but must preserve hazard evidence, affected scope, decision window, safety conditions, contractual impact, and post-event reconciliation.
exceptions: Buyer or winery response is conditional
recovery: Revise forecast or contract decision with provenance
field_validation_questions: What thresholds activate each hazard plan?
Who may override scheduled work and who must be notified?

overlays.name: Correction, supersession, and historical truth
overlays.operational_rule: A correction creates a new attributable version linked to the prior record; supersession changes which version is current without erasing what was known, communicated, approved, or acted on at the time.
exceptions: Correction occurs after submission, payroll, delivery, or payment
Prior recipients acted on stale data
source_anchors: source/Vineyard-Workflow-Discovery-report.md#L113-L115 — weigh entry review and manual correction
source/Vineyard-Workflow-Discovery-report.md#L151-L153 — contradictory numbers and post-submission corrections are field unknowns
field_validation_questions: Which corrections require approval or external resubmission?
How far downstream must a superseded value propagate?

overlays.name: Estate-grown and contract-fruit organizational boundary
overlays.operational_rule: Estate fruit may route authority inward, while contract fruit preserves both organizations, their contacts, identity mappings, requirements, decision rights, messages, evidence, and commercial consequences at every handoff.
exceptions: A changed requirement reaches only one organization
Commercial terms conflict with field reality
recovery: Issue an amendment or dispute record
field_validation_questions: Which estate workflows bypass grower-relations steps?
Which contract-fruit events are authoritative in each party's system?

overlays.name: Certification regime operational constraints
overlays.operational_rule: Certification is not a year-end archive task: regime-specific constraints, separation rules, allowed inputs, training, cleaning, transport, preparation, and evidence obligations alter ordinary work before, during, and after execution.
exceptions: Buffer crop mixed with certified crop
field_validation_questions: Which regime combinations apply to each block?
Which evidence must be collected at point of work rather than reconstructed later?

assumptions.statement: The ten canonical workflow IDs follow control/EXECUTION_PLAN.md even where the source report originally combines or splits adjacent topics differently.
assumptions.rationale: This keeps independent evidence extraction comparable during adjudication without treating the live taxonomy as source fact.

assumptions.statement: Workflow states are product-neutral operational lifecycle labels inferred from source sequences and required distinctions; they are not claimed as universal vineyard terminology.
assumptions.rationale: The source often documents obligations and handoffs but not formal state-machine names.

assumptions.statement: Exact approval thresholds, separation of duties, escalation timing, stale-data windows, and correction authority vary by organization, jurisdiction, buyer, and contract.
assumptions.rationale: The report repeatedly identifies role overlap and variation, and explicitly says behavioral details are the largest unknowns.

assumptions.statement: Offline synchronization behavior is modeled as an overlay requirement, not a confirmed current workflow.
assumptions.rationale: The source establishes scattered, stale, verbal, paper-first, and contradictory records but does not directly observe devices, connectivity, or conflict rates.

assumptions.statement: Correction and supersession are separated because a corrected value and the historical fact that an earlier value was communicated or acted upon can both matter operationally and commercially.
assumptions.rationale: Weigh-entry correction, forecast revision, audit correction, payroll uncertainty, and identity changes all require non-destructive history even though the report does not prescribe one shared mechanism.
